title: Quiz: Cosmic Rays and Ionizing Radiation
description: Test your understanding of cosmic ray impacts on quantum hardware and mitigation strategies

questions.question: When a cosmic ray deposits energy in a superconductor, what is the primary mechanism that affects qubit coherence?

options.text: Creation of quasiparticles that break Cooper pairs

questions.explanation: Cosmic ray energy deposition creates high-frequency phonons that break Cooper pairs, generating a burst of quasiparticles (typically ~10^6 per keV deposited). These quasiparticles cause T1 degradation as they diffuse across the chip.

questions.question: Approximately how many quasiparticles are created per keV of energy deposited in a superconductor?
questions.explanation: The number of quasiparticles is approximately N_qp = E_dep / (2*Delta), where Delta ~ 0.18 meV for aluminum. For 1 keV, this gives roughly 10^6 quasiparticles, accounting for pair-breaking efficiency.

questions.question: What is the typical diffusion range of quasiparticles on a superconducting chip?
questions.explanation: Quasiparticles diffuse with D ~ 20 cm^2/s and have lifetimes of ~100-1000 us. This gives a diffusion range of sqrt(D*tau) ~ 1-5 mm, meaning multiple nearby qubits can be affected by a single impact.

questions.question: Cosmic ray events violate the independent error assumption used in standard quantum error correction.
questions.explanation: True! Cosmic ray events cause correlated errors across multiple qubits that are physically close together. They also cause time-correlated errors during the recovery period. Standard QEC codes assume independent errors, so correlated bursts can exceed the effective error threshold.

questions.question: What is the approximate sea-level muon flux?
questions.explanation: The sea-level muon flux is approximately 0.01 particles per cm^2 per second (about 1 per cm^2 per 100 seconds). This means a 25 mm^2 chip experiences roughly one muon impact every few minutes.

questions.question: Which mitigation strategy can most effectively reduce cosmic ray impacts?

options.text: Operating the quantum computer underground (>100m depth)

questions.explanation: Underground operation dramatically reduces cosmic ray flux. At 100m depth, muon flux is reduced by ~10x; at 1000m, by ~1000x. Lead shielding helps with gamma rays but barely affects muons. Temperature and qubit frequency don't reduce the event rate, only potentially the recovery time.

questions.question: Which are valid software/hardware strategies for handling cosmic ray events? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Real-time T1 monitoring to detect events
Flagging and discarding affected measurement rounds
Active veto systems with scintillator detectors
Increasing qubit frequency to outrun the quasiparticles

questions.explanation: Valid strategies include: (a) T1 monitoring to detect sudden degradation, (b) flagging affected data rather than including corrupted measurements, and (c) active veto systems that trigger on particle detection. Increasing qubit frequency (d) doesn't help - the issue is quasiparticle poisoning, not the qubit operation speed.
